prompt: Bacteria that cause disease are called:

options.text: Pathogenic
Nonpathogenic only by definition
Saprophytes that only help
Viruses exclusively

explanation: Pathogenic microorganisms cause infection/disease; nonpathogenic are typically harmless or beneficial.

distractorExplanations.b: Nonpathogenic are not disease-causing by definition.
distractorExplanations.c: Saprophytes live on dead matter; not the term asked.

prompt: The body’s first line of defense against infection includes:

options.text: Ignoring handwashing
Healthy unbroken skin
Sharing needles in the salon
Working with open bleeding wounds uncovered on clients

explanation: Intact skin is a primary barrier; salon pros protect skin integrity.

prompt: A disinfectant used in salons must be:

options.text: Any household cleaner sprayed briefly without contact time
Soap and water alone for metal implements that require disinfection
EPA-registered and used according to the label for the claimed pathogens
Perfume to mask odors instead of disinfection

explanation: Contact time, concentration, and EPA registration matter for legally proper disinfection.

distractorExplanations.b: Cleaning precedes disinfection; soap alone isn’t hospital-level disinfection for many implements.

prompt: Cleaning (sanitation in older wording) differs from disinfection in that cleaning:

options.text: Always sterilizes to surgical zero
Is illegal in salons
Replaces the need for any disinfectant forever
Removes visible debris and reduces microbes but does not kill all pathogens like disinfection steps

explanation: Multi-step infection control: clean, then disinfect (or sterilize when required).

distractorExplanations.c: Disinfection still needed for many tools.

prompt: Sterilization:

options.text: Destroys all microbial life including spores (typically via autoclave methods in appropriate settings)
Is the same as dusting a station
Is achieved by a quick wipe with alcohol on hair
Means storing tools in an open drawer

explanation: Most salon implements are disinfected rather than sterilized; know the distinction for exams.

prompt: OSHA is primarily concerned with:

options.text: Setting state cosmetology practical exam scores only
Workplace safety and health of employees
Issuing beauty magazine ratings
Approving haircut fashion trends

explanation: OSHA covers bloodborne pathogens, hazard communication, SDS access, etc.

prompt: A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) provides:

options.text: Client fashion preferences
State board license numbers only
Hazard, handling, and emergency information about chemical products
Appointment book templates

explanation: SDS access is required for salon chemicals under Hazard Communication.

prompt: pH of hair and skin is generally:

options.text: Strongly alkaline like oven cleaner
Neutral exactly 10
pH 0 always
Slightly acidic

explanation: Acid mantle (~4.5–5.5) protects; strong alkalinity swells cuticle.

prompt: An alkaline permanent wave solution primarily:

options.text: Raises cuticle and breaks disulfide bonds to reform a new shape when processed/neutralized properly
Only adds shine without chemical change
Dyes hair permanently with pigment molecules only
Disinfects implements

explanation: Perm chemistry involves reduction of disulfide bonds; neutralization reforms bonds.

distractorExplanations.d: Not a disinfectant step for tools.

prompt: Hydrogen peroxide in haircolor developers acts as:

options.text: A reducing agent identical to thio
An oxidizing agent that helps swell the cuticle and develop color
A pure conditioner oil
A sterilant for skin

explanation: Volume of developer relates to lift capacity; misuse causes damage/burns.

distractorExplanations.a: Thio is a reducer in perms/relaxers contexts differently.

prompt: Blood exposure incident procedure includes:

options.text: Continue cutting and ignore bleeding
Use the same tissue on multiple clients
Stop service, glove, clean, apply antiseptic/bandage as appropriate, disinfect station, document per rules
Pour leftover color into the wound

explanation: State board blood exposure protocols protect client and licensee.

prompt: UV sanitizers in salons typically:

options.text: Replace EPA disinfection completely in all states always
Sterilize tools in 5 seconds guaranteed
Clean hair off brushes automatically
Store already cleaned/disinfected tools; they are not a substitute for proper disinfection alone in many rules

explanation: Know your state: UV cabinets are often storage, not primary disinfection.

distractorExplanations.c: Debris must be cleaned manually first.

prompt: A contraindication means:

options.text: A condition that makes a service inadvisable or requires physician referral
A product guaranteed for all clients
A type of shears
A booth rental contract only

explanation: Recognize scalp disease, open sores, infections—do not proceed inappropriately.

prompt: Parasites that can infest the scalp include:

options.text: Healthy terminal hair only
Head lice (pediculosis capitis)
Keratin protein alone
Melanin granules

explanation: Infestations are contagious; discontinue service and refer.

prompt: Inhalation of excessive aerosol products is best reduced by:

options.text: Spraying in unventilated closets only
Encouraging deep breaths of concentrate fumes
Adequate ventilation and directed spraying away from faces
Ignoring SDS warnings

explanation: Respiratory safety is part of professional practice.

prompt: The hair cuticle is the:

options.text: Outer protective layer of overlapping scales
Innermost medullary pith only
Living dermis under the scalp only
Sebaceous gland itself

explanation: Cuticle condition affects shine, porosity, and chemical service outcomes.

prompt: Porosity refers to:

options.text: The number of hairs per square inch only
The hair’s ability to absorb moisture
Natural hair color only
Curl pattern exclusively

explanation: Porosity guides chemical timing and product choice.

prompt: A blunt (one-length) haircut is characterized by:

options.text: Extreme short-to-long layering only
Shaving the scalp entirely
All hair cut to the same length weight line
Only razor tapering without a perimeter

explanation: Solid form creates a heavy weight line.

distractorExplanations.d: Blunt can be scissor-cut with a clear perimeter.

prompt: Elevation in haircutting controls:

options.text: Only the shampoo brand
Disinfectant contact time
License renewal dates
Layering / the degree of graduation away from the head shape

explanation: 0° elevation builds weight; higher elevation removes weight/layers.

prompt: Overdirection is used to:

options.text: Create length or shape by combing hair away from its natural fall before cutting
Disinfect shears
Measure peroxide volume
Cure scalp ringworm

explanation: Overdirection designs longer perimeters or disconnected shapes.

prompt: A patch test (predisposition test) for color is done to:

options.text: Measure hair density only
Check for allergic sensitivity before certain color services
Replace shampoo
Determine shears angle

explanation: Required/recommended for many oxidative color services per manufacturer and law.

prompt: Demi-permanent color typically:

options.text: Always lightens 4 levels like high-lift bleach
Permanently changes disulfide bonds like a relaxer
Deposits tone with little or no lift of natural pigment
Is identical to powdered lightener

explanation: Demi uses low/no ammonia systems for deposit; grey coverage varies by product.

prompt: When lightening virgin hair, a common approach is to:

options.text: Always apply to scalp first and leave longest without regard to heat
Mix lightener with tap water only as universal rule
Skip strand tests always
Apply to mid-lengths/ends first where resistant, then scalp last due to body heat (per product instructions)

explanation: Body heat accelerates scalp processing; follow manufacturer instructions.

prompt: A relaxer that is too strong or poorly timed can cause:

options.text: Hair breakage and scalp burns
Stronger disulfide bonds automatically
Guaranteed healthier cuticles
Sterile tools

explanation: Base application, timing, and smooth tension control risk.

distractorExplanations.b: Bonds are broken/reformed carefully—damage occurs if misused.

prompt: Neutralizer in permanent waving:

options.text: Is optional if the client is in a hurry
Rebuilds disulfide bonds in the new shape (oxidation step)
Only shampoos without chemical role
Removes all melanin

explanation: Skipping neutralization leaves hair weak and bonds incomplete.

prompt: Texture services should not proceed when:

options.text: Hair is slightly dry
Client wants a trim later
Scalp has open sores, abrasions, or contagious conditions
The salon music is soft

explanation: Contraindications protect client health; refer when needed.

distractorExplanations.a: Dryness may need conditioning—not absolute stop like infection.

prompt: Slide cutting is generally used to:

options.text: Sterilize the comb
Apply relaxer base
Measure porosity scientifically only
Blend and remove bulk with a mobile shear technique

explanation: Texturizing methods include point cutting, notching, slide cutting—use appropriately on suitable hair.

prompt: Guideline in haircutting means:

options.text: The first section cut that subsequent sections are matched to
The EPA disinfectant label only
The client consultation card fee
The booth lease clause

explanation: Stationary vs traveling guidelines create different shapes.

prompt: Highlighting differs from all-over color primarily because it:

options.text: Never uses lightener
Lightens selected strands for dimension rather than a single uniform deposit/lift on all hair
Is only done on beards by law
Requires no sectioning

explanation: Foiling/balayage/cap methods isolate strands.

prompt: A binder/filler in color correction may be used to:

options.text: Disinfect shears
Replace shampoo bowls
Equalize porosity and replace missing underlying pigment before depositing tone
Cure alopecia areata

explanation: Color correction theory uses fillers to prevent muddy/uneven results on porous hair.

prompt: The epidermis is:

options.text: The outermost layer of the skin
The subcutaneous fat only
A type of nail plate fungus exclusively
The salon’s reception desk

explanation: Epidermis includes the stratum corneum barrier important in esthetic services.

prompt: A pustule is:

options.text: Healthy unbroken skin
An inflamed papule containing pus
A hair cutting angle
A type of shears

explanation: Recognize lesions; do not service contagious or contraindicated conditions.

prompt: During a basic facial, cleansing is performed to:

options.text: Replace extractions of all clients forcefully
Sterilize the dermis surgically
Remove makeup, oil, and debris before further steps
Color the hair

explanation: Follow client skin analysis; avoid contraindicated machines/products.

prompt: Massage movements should generally be directed:

options.text: Random digging into the eye orbits
Across open herpetic lesions
With dirty hands after phone use without hygiene
From insertion toward origin for many manipulative strokes on the face (as taught), with appropriate pressure

explanation: Classic theory: manipulate toward origin; never work on contagious lesions.

prompt: The nail plate is composed mainly of:

options.text: Keratin
Living fat cells only
Melanin exclusively
Calcium metal rods

explanation: Nail is hardened keratin; matrix produces the plate.

distractorExplanations.c: Melanin pigments skin/hair more than nail plate bulk.

prompt: Onychomycosis is:

options.text: Healthy nail growth
A fungal infection of the nail
A hair porosity test
A type of perm rod

explanation: Do not work on infected nails; refer to a physician.

prompt: Before a manicure, implements should be:

options.text: Wiped on the stylist’s pants only
Shared from a contaminated jar of “community tools” without disinfection
Cleaned and disinfected (or disposable single-use) per state board rules
Rinsed with soda water only as sterilization

explanation: Nail services have high blood-exposure potential—strict infection control.

prompt: Cuticle remover chemicals are generally:

options.text: Strong acids identical to bleach powders
EPA sterilants for skin
Used to lighten hair 4 levels
Alkaline products that soften pterygium/cuticle tissue—use carefully and avoid excessive living tissue removal

explanation: Do not cut living tissue illegally/unsafely; many states restrict invasive cuticle cutting.

prompt: Acrylic (liquid/powder) nail enhancements require:

options.text: Proper ratio, ventilation, and never applying over fungus/infection
Application on wet undried oils for better adhesion always
No dust control ever
Ignoring manufacturer instructions

explanation: Monomer vapor and dust safety matter; enhancement over infection is contraindicated.

distractorExplanations.b: Oils impair adhesion; prep is dry/clean.

prompt: A client with inflamed, swollen nail folds (paronychia signs) should be:

options.text: Pushed aggressively into the eponychium
Referred for medical evaluation; do not perform invasive nail services
Soaked in shared unclean water then ignored
Covered with acrylic to “seal” infection

explanation: Infection is a stop-service situation.

prompt: Hard wax hair removal differs from soft wax in that hard wax typically:

options.text: Requires a cloth strip identically always
Is used only on scalp perms
Is applied thicker and removed without a strip when set
Disinfects implements

explanation: Hard wax suits sensitive areas for many estheticians; follow contraindications (retinoids, sunburn, etc.).

prompt: Tweezing is performed:

options.text: Across contagious impetigo lesions
With shared tweezers uncleaned between clients
On moles that change rapidly without referral consideration
In the direction of hair growth with proper skin support and sanitation of tweezers

explanation: Sanitize tweezers between clients; avoid suspicious lesions.

prompt: Massage is contraindicated when:

options.text: Client has contagious skin disease, thrombosis concerns, or other conditions flagged in consultation
Client simply wants relaxation and is healthy
Skin is clean and intact with no red flags
You have completed hygiene and analysis appropriately

explanation: Health history screens for massage contraindications.

distractorExplanations.c: Intact clean skin can be appropriate.

prompt: The eponychium is:

options.text: The free edge tip only
Living skin at the base of the nail plate (often confused with cuticle)
A hair medulla
A disinfectant brand

explanation: Avoid cutting living eponychium; remove only non-living cuticle tissue as allowed by law.

prompt: Pedicure basins must be:

options.text: Just refilled with fresh water over debris
Shared without disinfection all day
Cleaned and disinfected between clients per state rules (including jets/filters if applicable)
Ignored if the water looks clear

explanation: Whirlpool pedicure spas have been linked to infections when improperly disinfected.

distractorExplanations.a: Debris must be removed then disinfected.

prompt: A cosmetology license generally allows the holder to:

options.text: Perform services defined in state law for which they are licensed, in a licensed establishment when required
Practice medicine and prescribe drugs
Ignore all infection-control rules
Work without any license if tipped in cash

explanation: Scope is state-specific; working outside scope risks discipline.

prompt: Practicing with an expired license is:

options.text: Encouraged to save money
Illegal / subject to penalties in most jurisdictions
Allowed if the client consents verbally only
Fine if posted on Instagram

explanation: Renew on time; reciprocity rules vary by state.

distractorExplanations.c: Client consent doesn’t legalize unlicensed practice.

prompt: Client records for chemical services often should include:

options.text: Other clients’ credit card CVV codes shared openly
Staff Social Security numbers
Formulas, processing times, patch test info, and release/consultation notes as required
Nothing—memory is enough legally always

explanation: Documentation protects client and licensee in disputes/allergies.

distractorExplanations.b: Never store staff SSNs in client cards.

prompt: Booth rental / independent contractor status means:

options.text: The salon owner is always your W-2 employer automatically
Licenses are unnecessary
Disinfection is optional for renters
You may be responsible for your own taxes, insurance, and compliance as a separate business—know state rules

explanation: Misclassification and sanitation responsibilities still apply to the licensee.

distractorExplanations.a: IC vs employee depends on facts/law.

prompt: Advertising that you can cure skin cancer in the salon would be:

options.text: Illegal/unprofessional and outside scope of practice
Standard cosmetology marketing
Required by OSHA
A type of haircut

explanation: Do not make medical claims; refer suspicious lesions.

prompt: Single-use items such as orangewood sticks and disposable files should be:

options.text: Disinfected and reused indefinitely always
Discarded after one client
Shared in a community cup
Rinsed and returned to the clean drawer as sterile

explanation: Porous single-use items cannot be properly disinfected for reuse in many rules.

prompt: A state board inspector may check for:

options.text: Your personal streaming passwords
Only the color of the walls regardless of sanitation
Proper licensure posting, disinfection practices, and establishment compliance
Whether tips were reported to friends

explanation: Be inspection-ready every day.

distractorExplanations.d: Tax issues differ from board sanitation focus.

prompt: Continuing education requirements:

options.text: Never exist in any U.S. state
Replace the need for a primary license
Are set by fashion magazines only
Vary by state and may be required for license renewal

explanation: Know your state’s CE and renewal rules.

prompt: Refusal of service is appropriate when:

options.text: A contagious condition or contraindication makes the service unsafe/illegal to perform
The client’s protected class status is the sole reason in a discriminatory way
You dislike a client’s music taste only, violating public accommodation unlawfully
You want to keep a deposit without policy after refusing without cause unlawfully

explanation: Safety refusals are valid; unlawful discrimination is not.

distractorExplanations.b: Civil rights laws apply to public accommodations.
distractorExplanations.c: Taste alone isn’t a safety refusal.

prompt: Proper storage of disinfected tools is typically:

options.text: Loose in an open drawer with used hair and debris
In a clean, covered container labeled as clean/disinfected
In the employee lunch bag
Soaking overnight in bleach without measuring, unlabeled

explanation: Avoid recontamination after disinfection; follow wet disinfectant label rules.

prompt: Mixing chemicals from different manufacturers without guidance can be dangerous because:

options.text: It always improves EPA registration
It is required weekly by OSHA
Reactions may release toxic gases or reduce effectiveness unpredictably
It sterilizes the air

explanation: Follow labels; never mix bleach with ammonia, etc.

prompt: Informed consent / consultation helps:

options.text: Eliminate the need for patch tests when required
Allow unlicensed assistants to perform restricted services
Skip disinfection
Set expectations, reveal allergies/contraindications, and document agreement to the service plan

explanation: Good consultations reduce liability and improve results.

prompt: Price advertising that shows a service “from $20” must generally be:

options.text: Truthful and not bait-and-switch deceptive under consumer protection principles
Intentionally false to get clients in the door
Hidden in 2-point font only as best practice for deception
Unrelated to ethics

explanation: Professional ethics and laws prohibit deceptive advertising.

prompt: An apprentice/student working on the public typically must:

options.text: Work unsupervised as a fully licensed professional without credentials
Be supervised as required by state law and stay within student/apprentice scope
Skip infection control until licensed
Perform medical diagnosis

explanation: Training pathways are regulated; public protection is the goal.

distractorExplanations.c: Infection control applies in school clinics too.

prompt: If a product is recalled for safety, the salon should:

options.text: Use it up quickly before anyone notices
Relabel it as a different brand
Stop use, follow recall instructions, and protect clients
Ignore manufacturer notices

explanation: Consumer safety overrides inventory loss concerns.
